citations.snippet_text: RNN LMs + Transformer LMs 1 10-301/10-601 Introduction to Machine Learning Pat Virtue & Matt Gormley Lecture 18 Mar. 18, 2026 Machine Learning Department School of Computer Science Carnegie Mellon University
ATTENTION The key building block for Transformer language models 82
TRANSFORMER LANGUAGE MODELS Generative Pretrained Transformers (GPT) 103

reply_sections.heading: What is a Transformer?
reply_sections.text: A Transformer is a type of language model, often referred to as a Transformer Language Model (LM) or Generative Pretrained Transformer (GPT) in the context of machine learning. The language model component of a Transformer LM functions similarly to an RNN-LM. A key building block for Transformer language models is attention. Each layer within a Transformer LM is comprised of several sublayers, which include attention, a feed-forward neural network, layer normalization, and residual connections.

messages.text: what is the formula for vc dimension

citations.snippet_text: Sample Complexity Results 18 Realizable Agnostic Four Cases we care about… For these two cases, we will use a new definition for the “complexity” of a Hypothesis space called VC Dimension

Intuition for VC Dimension 24 ●For “nice” infinite hypothesis sets ℋ, many hypotheses in ℋwill behave similarly ●Relative to this dataset, these two hypotheses are identical! Slide from Henry Chai & Geoff Gordon

Intuition for VC Dimension 25 ●For “nice” infinite hypothesis sets ℋ, many hypotheses in ℋwill behave similarly ●Relative to this dataset, these two hypotheses are identical! Idea: instead of using full size of ℋ, count how many actually distinct hypotheses there are Slide from Henry Chai & Geoff Gordon

reply_sections.heading: VC Dimension
reply_sections.text: The provided lecture materials introduce VC Dimension as a definition for the "complexity" of a Hypothesis space, particularly for realizable and agnostic cases, and for "nice" infinite hypothesis sets. It is described as an idea to count how many actually distinct hypotheses there are, rather than using the full size of the hypothesis set. However, the lecture slides do not provide a specific formula for calculating VC Dimension.

reply_sections.heading: Basic Neurotransmitter Functions
reply_sections.text: Neurotransmitters function by binding to receptors and activating them, which in turn produces a biological effect or signal. For example, dopamine can inhibit 'stop' cells and excite 'go' cells to increase movement, cause pleasure in the brain's reward system, and promote attention, thinking, and self-control. Serotonin signals are involved in mood and can be extended to help with anxiety and depression.

citations.snippet_text: ​Concordance vs. Comorbidity​ ​Concordance: relatives tend to share the same disorder at higher-than-chance rates.​ ​Comorbidity: two disorders occur together in the same person more often than expected.​ ​Similarity: both suggest some kind of relationship.​ ​Difference: concordance is about shared disorder in relativ…

questions.expected_answer: ​Concordance vs. Comorbidity​ ​Concordance: relatives tend to share the same disorder at higher-than-chance rates.​ ​Comorbidity: two disorders occur together in the same person more often than expected.​ ​Similarity: both suggest some kind of relationship.​ ​Difference: concordance is about shared disorder in relatives; comorbidity is about two disorders in one individual.​ ​Expected comorbidity if independent:​​P(A and B) =​​P(A) × P(B)​ ​Calculating observed comorbidity as follows:​ ​-​ ​Overall comorbidity rate = percent of all people with comorbid A&B = number_of_people_with_comorbid_A&B /​ ​total_number_of_people_in_whole_set​ ​-​ ​Comorbidity rate in disease A = percent of people with disease A who have comorbid A&B =​ ​number_of_people_with_comorbid_A&B / total_number_of_people_with_A​ ​-​ ​Comorbidity rate in disease B = percent of people with disease B who have comorbid A&B =​ ​number_of_people_with_comorbid_A&B / total_number_of_people_with_B​ ​-​ ​Assuming that one of the diseases always comes first, we will simplify our lives in this class by considering the two most common​ ​explanations for a correlation between two diseases:​ ​1)​ ​A single set of genes gives rise to risk of both disease 1 and disease 2.

student_context.goals:
Compensation model (why SSRIs take time to work).
5-HT receptor differences (especially 5-HT1 vs 5-HT2).
Tolerance vs withdrawal mechanisms (opioids, cocaine, etc).
Applying concepts to explanation-style questions.
student_context.known:
Basic neurotransmitter functions (dopamine, serotonin, GABA).
General idea of SSRIs and that they increase serotonin.
Know symptoms of major disorders like MDD and GAD.
student_context.weak_areas:
Compensation model (why SSRIs take time to work).
5-HT receptor differences (especially 5-HT1 vs 5-HT2).
Tolerance vs withdrawal mechanisms (opioids, cocaine, etc).
Applying concepts to explanation-style questions.

messages.text: Why does the update use subtraction instead of addition?

citations.snippet_text: Subtract the gradient to reduce the loss.

reply_sections.heading: Lecture-grounded explanation
reply_sections.text: The lecture slide writes the update as w <- w - eta * dL/dw, which means the parameter moves opposite the gradient so the loss decreases rather than increases.

quality_summary.notes: Short student notes with a few paraphrases

bullets: Cache activations in the forward pass
Track the sign on the learning update
Compare each step with the slide deck
slides.snippet_text: Remember to save intermediate activations, keep the minus sign in the update, and check each derivative term.
slides.title: Student recap

quality_summary.notes: Lecture deck imported cleanly in fixture mode

bullets: Explain why gradients are propagated from the loss backward
Connect local derivatives to global updates
Identify the role of the learning rate
slides.snippet_text: Learning objectives: explain the chain rule, compute a backward pass, and apply gradient descent updates.
slides.title: Backpropagation Essentials

bullets: Write the output as a composition of intermediate functions
Differentiate one edge at a time
Multiply local gradients to get the total derivative
slides.snippet_text: The chain rule breaks a complicated derivative into local derivatives that can be multiplied along the computation path.
slides.title: Chain Rule Intuition

bullets: Forward pass computes activations and loss
Backward pass reuses cached activations
Each layer passes gradient information to the previous layer
slides.snippet_text: Store intermediate activations during the forward pass so the backward pass can reuse them when computing gradients.
slides.title: Forward Pass and Backward Pass

bullets: Use the learning rate eta to control step size
Subtract the gradient to reduce the loss
Check tensor shapes before applying the update
slides.snippet_text: Parameter update: w <- w - eta * dL/dw. The minus sign matters because we are descending the loss surface.
slides.title: Gradient Descent Update

bullets: Do not skip intermediate terms in the chain rule
Do not add the gradient when the slide uses subtraction
Verify activation and weight matrix dimensions
slides.snippet_text: Common errors include dropping an intermediate derivative, reversing the sign of the update, and ignoring dimension checks.
slides.title: Common Mistakes

quality_summary.notes: Template favors short prompts followed by concise rubric bullets

bullets: Explain / Compare / Derive prompt style
Compact answer format
Rubric awards points for named steps
slides.snippet_text: Each question begins with an imperative verb and expects two to three compact supporting points.
slides.title: Template structure

coverage_report.notes: The fixture practice set concentrates on the core worked-example slides rather than the title slide.

citations.snippet_text: Multiply local gradients to get the total derivative.

questions.expected_answer: A strong answer states that the total derivative is built by multiplying local derivatives along the computation path, usually mentioning intermediate activations or nodes.

rubric.description: Names local derivatives and explains that they are multiplied
Links the chain rule directly to gradient flow through layers

questions.scoring_guide_text: Full credit requires both the mathematical idea and the network-specific interpretation.
questions.stem: Explain how the chain rule supports backpropagation through a multi-layer network.

citations.snippet_text: Store intermediate activations during the forward pass so the backward pass can reuse them.

questions.expected_answer: A strong answer describes the forward pass computing activations and the loss, then explains that the backward pass uses those stored activations to compute gradients layer by layer.

rubric.description: Explains what is computed and saved
Explains how saved activations support gradient computation

questions.scoring_guide_text: Look for explicit mention of reusing forward-pass information during the backward pass.
questions.stem: Walk through a forward pass and backward pass, explaining why cached activations are needed in the backward computation.

citations.snippet_text: Parameter update: w <- w - eta * dL/dw.

questions.expected_answer: The answer should give w <- w - eta * dL/dw and say the minus sign moves parameters opposite the gradient so the loss decreases.

rubric.description: Writes the update rule accurately
Explains that subtraction corresponds to descending the loss

questions.scoring_guide_text: Students should connect the sign to optimization, not only memorize the formula.
questions.stem: State the gradient descent update rule from the lecture and explain the role of the minus sign.

student_context.goals: Prepare for a closed-book midterm practice section

citations.snippet_text: Do not skip intermediate terms in the chain rule.

common_mistakes.pattern: Skipping an intermediate derivative in the chain rule
common_mistakes.prevention_advice: Label every edge in the computation path and verify that each local derivative appears exactly once.
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: Students jump from the loss directly to a parameter derivative and forget one local edge.

citations.snippet_text: Parameter update: w <- w - eta * dL/dw. The minus sign matters.

common_mistakes.pattern: Reversing the sign in the gradient descent update
common_mistakes.prevention_advice: Say out loud that the update subtracts the gradient because the goal is to descend the loss surface.
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: The update can look like a formula to memorize instead of a loss-reduction step.

citations.snippet_text: Verify activation and weight matrix dimensions.

common_mistakes.pattern: Ignoring shape checks during backward updates
common_mistakes.prevention_advice: Check each tensor shape before the update and compare it to the corresponding weight dimensions.
common_mistakes.why_it_happens: A derivative can be numerically plausible even when its dimensions do not match the weight matrix.

citations.snippet_text: The chain rule breaks a complicated derivative into local derivatives that can be multiplied along the computation path.

prerequisites.why_needed: Backpropagation multiplies local derivatives along the computation path.

citations.snippet_text: Store intermediate activations during the forward pass so the backward pass can reuse them.

prerequisites.why_needed: The backward pass reuses cached values from the forward pass when computing gradients.

citations.snippet_text: Parameter update: w <- w - eta * dL/dw.

prerequisites.why_needed: You need the update rule to translate the gradient into a parameter change.

citations.snippet_text: Differentiate one edge at a time, then multiply local gradients.

study_sequence.objective: Rewrite the chain rule in your own notation and connect each local derivative to one edge in the computation graph.
tasks: Annotate slide 2 with your own symbols
Say aloud what each local derivative means
Write one worked derivative without looking
study_sequence.title: Rebuild the chain rule from one slide

citations.snippet_text: Store intermediate activations during the forward pass so the backward pass can reuse them.

study_sequence.objective: Use the lecture example to match stored activations from the forward pass to each gradient in the backward pass.
tasks: Label where each gradient is computed
study_sequence.title: Trace a full forward/backward pass

citations.snippet_text: Subtract the gradient to reduce the loss, and check tensor shapes before applying the update.

study_sequence.objective: Practice checking the minus sign and tensor shapes before applying an update to weights.
tasks: Write the update rule from memory
Test one example with the wrong sign and explain why it fails
Check the shape of each gradient term
study_sequence.title: Audit the update sign and dimensions
